title: Answering Tricky PMP Questions
summary: Most PMP questions are scenarios with several plausible answers. A repeatable method makes the 'best' choice clearer.

sections.heading: Situational questions
sections.body: A scenario asks for the best action and every option looks reasonable. Read for three things: the project phase, the real underlying problem, and who is involved. Then choose the response a strong project manager would take.
sections.tip: Pick the response that is proactive, collaborative, and within the PM's authority.

sections.heading: 'What should you do first / next?'
sections.body: These test sequence. The usual order is: understand and analyse the situation, communicate with the team or stakeholders, review or update the plan, and only then act. Documenting and investigating almost always come before acting or escalating.
sections.tip: First steps are usually investigate, gather information, or review the plan — rarely 'escalate' or 'implement now'.

sections.heading: Eliminate down to two
sections.body:
Cross out the obvious losers first:

- Extreme actions — fire someone, ignore it, blindly comply, go straight to the sponsor.
- Options that take you out of the loop or abdicate responsibility.

You are usually left with two reasonable answers; choose the more proactive, value- and people-focused one.
sections.tip: Delete anything reactive, extreme, or that hands off the PM's responsibility — then decide between what remains.

sections.heading: Read for predictive vs agile cues
sections.body: The wording tells you which approach applies. Baseline, change control board, and WBS point to predictive. Sprint, backlog, iteration, and Product Owner point to agile. A mix means hybrid. The best answer matches the approach in the scenario.
sections.tip: Match your answer to the implied approach — an agile fix to a predictive question (or vice versa) is usually wrong.

source: PM Cram study notes — reworded for the current ECO-based PMP exam.
